perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall obtain and maintain, for the Term (i) commercial general liability insurance with a single combined liability limit of at least $5,000,000 per occurrence, (ii) workers compensation/employer's liability insurance with a liability limit of at least $1,000,000 per occurrence or, if greater, the statutory minimum, and (iii) "all risk" property insurance on a replacement cost basis adequate to cover all assets and business interruption Losses that a Party may suffer in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, subject to policy limits, and in the case of the policies described in clause (i) above, naming the other Party as an additional insured thereunder. Upon request, each Party shall provide the other Party a certificate of insurance as proof of insurance coverage.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall obtain and maintain, for the Term (i) commercial general liability insurance, (ii) workers compensation/employer's liability insurance, and (iii) property insurance, subject to policy limits. Each Party may provide the other Party a certificate of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific liability limits for commercial general liability and workers' compensation insurance, and by removing the 'all risk' and 'replacement cost basis' requirement for property insurance the modified text contradicts the requirement to maintain adequate insurance coverage, increasing risk. Removing the obligation to provide the certificate of insurance also contributes to this issue.
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois law (215 ILCS 5/143.13) sets minimum requirements for insurance policies. While this specific section focuses on auto insurance, it establishes the principle that insurance policies must meet certain minimum standards. By removing the specific coverage amounts and the 'all risk' requirement, the modified text allows for policies that might not adequately cover potential losses, contradicting the intent of ensuring sufficient financial protection as mandated by the state's insurance regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 215 ILCS 5/ Illinois Insurance Code. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes essential information regarding insurance coverage limits, potentially leading to inadequate financial protection, which contradicts the intent of ensuring sufficient coverage. The original text specifies minimum liability limits for commercial general liability and workers' compensation insurance, along with requiring 'all risk' and 'replacement cost basis' for property insurance. The modified text eliminates these specific requirements. The Law Explanation suggests that insurance policies must meet certain minimum standards to ensure sufficient financial protection, supporting the idea that removing the coverage amounts contradicts this intent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the existence of the Illinois Insurance Code, implying there are regulations concerning insurance, but provides no specific details to further support or refute the contradiction.

perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for written notice and specifying the acceptable methods of delivery (in person, facsimile, email, or registered mail), the modified text creates ambiguity regarding how legal notices are to be served. This omission may contradict Illinois rules of civil procedure regarding service of process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Rules of Civil Procedure - Rule 105
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois Supreme Court Rule 105 outlines specific requirements for service of process. While this agreement isn't exactly service of process it details legal notices between parties to a contract. By omitting the requirement for 'written notice' this modified section introduces a legal risk. The law requires a specific method, whereas the modified contract does not guarantee this.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for written notice and the specific methods of delivery, potentially contradicting legal requirements for service of process or legal notices, which often mandate specific methods to ensure proper notification. While the provided snippet is limited, the Law Explanation suggests that Illinois law requires specific methods for legal notices, and the Perturbation Explanation highlights the ambiguity created by omitting the 'written notice' requirement. The omission could lead to disputes over whether proper notice was given, as the changed text doesn't guarantee a verifiable method of delivery.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party covenants and agrees that, without any additional consideration, it shall execute and deliver, or shall cause its Affiliates to execute and deliver, such documents and other papers and shall take, or shall cause its Affiliates to take, such further actions as may be reasonably required to carry out the provisions of this Agreement and give effect to the transactions contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party covenants and agrees that it shall take such further actions as may be required to carry out the provisions of this Agreement and give effect to the transactions contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to 'execute and deliver... such documents and other papers' and 'cause its Affiliates to execute and deliver, such documents and other papers,' the modified text removes the legal certainty that the parties will provide the necessary formal documentation to fulfill the agreement. This could contradict the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) requirements for written agreements, especially in transactions involving the sale of goods or secured transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-201, the statute of frauds provision, requires contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more to be in writing and signed to be enforceable. While this Transition Services Agreement isn't strictly a sale of goods, it involves multiple transactions that could fall under UCC Article 2 if the 'services' involve the provision of goods (e.g., IT equipment, software licenses). By removing the assurance that parties will 'execute and deliver... such documents and other papers,' the altered clause increases the risk that transactions under this agreement lack the legally required written form to be enforced under the UCC, particularly in Illinois (which has adopted the UCC).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-201. Formal Requirements; Statute of Frauds. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable by way of action or defense unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made between the parties and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought or by his authorized agent or broker. A writing is not insufficient because it omits or incorrectly states a term agreed upon but the contract is not enforceable under this paragraph beyond the quantity of goods shown in such writing. (2) Between merchants if within a reasonable time a writing in confirmation of the contract and sufficient against the sender is received and the party receiving it has reason to know its contents, it satisfies the requirements of subsection (1) against such party unless written notice of objection to its contents is given within 10 days after it is received. (3) A contract which does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (1) but which is valid in other respects is enforceable (a) if the goods are to be specially manufactured for the buyer and are not suitable for sale to others in the ordinary course of the seller's business and the seller, before notice of repudiation is received and under circumstances which reasonably indicate that the goods are for the buyer, has made either a substantial beginning of their manufacture or commitments for their procurement; or (b) if the party against whom enforcement is sought admits in his pleading, testimony or otherwise in court that a contract for sale was made, but the contract is not enforceable under this provision beyond the quantity of goods admitted; or (c) with respect to goods for which payment has been made and accepted or which have been received and

perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement for each party to 'execute and deliver... such documents and other papers' directly contradicts the statute of frauds provision under UCC § 2-201. The original text ensures that the parties fulfill the necessary formal documentation, whereas the changed text removes this assurance. The UCC § 2-201 states that '...a contract for the sale of goods for the price of $500 or more is not enforceable... unless there is some writing sufficient to indicate that a contract for sale has been made...' By removing the obligation to provide formal documentation, the modified text increases the risk that transactions under the agreement may lack the legally required written form to be enforceable. This removal undermines the need for a written contract as required by law.
